title: Policy clarification for verilator_ext_tests
body:
Note: seems like issues are disabled for https://github.com/verilator/verilator_ext_tests so opening this one here.

The README there says:

> To be accepted in this package, an external submodule tested here must:
> 
> Be under an open source license.
> Be available as a git repository.
> Be self checking (potentially checked by the t/ script you write).
> Be Verilator lint clean (using lint_off pragmas in code is fine).
> Be willing to consider patches as needed (i.e. not a "dead project").
> Already run CI against a fixed version of Verilator

I think the verilator_ext_tests repo is very useful, and I think it could be even more helpful, but I have some issues with the above requirements

> Be available as a git repository.

This is kind of fair, but a git repo in and of itself is not always sufficient. I for example would like to add https://github.com/OpenXiangShan/XiangShan, but that project uses Chisel with a ton of dependencies that would be unfortunate to add to the test suite dependency. At the same time, one can just check in the generated Verilog and test Verilator against that. This project has found at least 2 bugs that did not show through the test suite.

Also, both XiangShan and OpenTitan require test executables for the simulated CPUs that are not part of the tree, and require the target toolchain, but are not too hard to conjure up once and commit.

> Be Verilator lint clean (using lint_off pragmas in code is fine).

I'm not sure why this is important, for example OpenTitan has tons of warnings, but is a very helpful test project. It's ok if it's a lint warning that indicates we will produce broken code or undefined behaviour, we don't want to be backward compatible with that, but things like width warnings that are not harmful or UNOPTFLAT should not cause a project to be rejected.

> Be willing to consider patches as needed (i.e. not a "dead project").

Somewhat of fair, but a big bag of Verilog that has a defined expected behaviour is still helpful for finding non-trivial issues.

> Already run CI against a fixed version of Verilator

Not sure why this is important

---

I would like to propose that we allow anything that is:
- Open source (so we can lawfully copy it's code if needed),
- and self checking,
- and either
  - Available as a git submodule without too many extraneous dependencies
  - or, can be committed into verilator_ext_tests or some other submodule under our control as a blob

We don't really *need to* ever update the bag of code type of tests. We just want to make sure the bag of code does the same thing before or after a Verilator change.
